params.text:
Choice, control and independence

Dignity and independence are closely linked. When people keep control over their own lives — even in small ways — they keep their sense of self. Standard 7 asks you to support people's right to make choices and to take an active part in their own care.

  • Offer real choices: what to wear, what to eat, when to get up, whether to have a bath or a shower.
  • Support people to do things for themselves wherever they can, rather than doing everything for them "to save time".
  • Respect preferences and routines — the small habits that make someone feel like themselves.
  • Involve people in decisions about their care, and honour choices even when they differ from your own.

Doing with a person rather than for them takes patience, but it protects both their independence and their dignity.

params.alt: An older woman using her mobile phone independently in her own living room

params.question: It is more respectful to quickly dress someone yourself than to let them slowly do the parts they are able to do, because it saves them effort.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Taking over removes the person's independence and control. Good practice is to support people to do what they can for themselves, even if it takes longer.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Doing everything for someone to save time removes their independence. Supporting them to do what they can, at their own pace, is more dignified.
